additionalTime: 48 to 72 hours dough fermentation plus 3 hours dough warm-up
author: Robert Carr
description: A large 15- to 16-inch white pizza built for a Baking Steel Pro, with a 72-hour cold-fermented crust, garlic-ricotta mascarpone base, porcini-boosted browned mixed mushrooms, caramelized onion, fontina, whole-milk mozzarella, Pecorino Romano, torn speck, and lemon-chive finishing oil.
equipment: Baking Steel Pro or 16 x 16-inch baking steel
Covered dough container or large zip-top bag
Large cast-iron skillet or stainless steel skillet
Spice grinder or mortar and pestle, optional if using whole dried porcini mushrooms
Room-temperature rimless baking sheet, for finishing if the bottom crust browns early

groceryIngredients.display: high-protein bread flour, preferably Central Milling, King Arthur Sir Lancelot, or another strong bread flour

groceryIngredients.note: Use 15 grams in the scratch dough for extra crust flavor; replace with more bread flour if you want the easiest stretch.

groceryIngredients.display: Baking Steel 72 Hour Pizza Dough Mix pack, optional dough shortcut
groceryIngredients.note: Buy one pack if using instead of scratch dough; add 1 3/4 cups water to the mix, ferment as directed, then portion 450 to 500 grams prepared dough for one large 15- to 16-inch pizza.

groceryIngredients.display: fine sea salt, Morton Coarse Kosher Salt, or Diamond Crystal kosher salt
mixed mushrooms, such as cremini, shiitake, oyster, or maitake
groceryIngredients.note: Buy 12 ounces total. A mix with at least 4 ounces shiitake, oyster, or maitake gives a deeper flavor than all button mushrooms.

groceryIngredients.display: dried porcini mushrooms or porcini powder
groceryIngredients.note: Buy a small package; the recipe uses 1 teaspoon finely ground porcini powder and the rest keeps well for future sauces, soups, and pizzas.

groceryIngredients.display: medium yellow onion or sweet onion
sherry vinegar or red wine vinegar
Italian fontina or fontina Val d'Aosta

groceryIngredients.note: Speck gives the smokiest finish; prosciutto is the easiest substitute. Buy 3 ounces if sliced to order; the recipe uses 2 ounces.

ingredients: 285 grams high-protein bread flour, preferably Central Milling, King Arthur Sir Lancelot, or another strong bread flour
15 grams stone-ground whole wheat flour, or 15 grams more bread flour for the easiest stretch
195 grams cool water, about 3/4 cup plus 1 tablespoon plus 1/2 teaspoon
1/2 teaspoon honey for the dough
Scant 1/8 teaspoon instant yeast, about 0.4 grams
8 grams salt for the dough: about 1 1/2 level teaspoons fine sea salt, 1 3/4 level teaspoons Morton Coarse Kosher Salt, or 2 1/2 level teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt
1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil for the dough, plus 1 teaspoon for the dough container
1 to 2 tablespoons semolina flour or fine cornmeal, for dusting the peel
Optional dough shortcut: 450 to 500 grams prepared dough from Baking Steel 72 Hour Pizza Dough Mix for one large 15- to 16-inch pizza
12 ounces mixed mushrooms, such as cremini, shiitake, oyster, or maitake, cleaned and sliced 1/4 inch thick
1 teaspoon porcini powder or finely ground dried porcini mushrooms
1 medium yellow onion or sweet onion, about 6 to 7 ounces, thinly sliced
3 tablespoons plus 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil, divided
2 tablespoons unsalted butter, divided
3 small garlic cloves, finely grated or minced, divided
1 teaspoon fresh thyme leaves, plus 2 small thyme sprigs if available
1/2 teaspoon fine sea salt or Morton Coarse Kosher Salt for the onion and mushrooms, plus a small pinch for the white base
1/4 teaspoon black pepper for the mushrooms, plus 1/8 teaspoon for the white base and 1/8 teaspoon for finishing
1 teaspoon sherry vinegar or red wine vinegar for the caramelized onion
1/3 cup whole-milk ricotta, drained for 15 minutes if loose or wet
2 tablespoons mascarpone
1 1/2 ounces Pecorino Romano, finely grated, divided
1/2 teaspoon finely grated lemon zest, divided
5 ounces low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella from a block, freshly shredded
3 ounces Italian fontina or fontina Val d'Aosta, finely diced or shredded
2 ounces thinly sliced speck or prosciutto, torn into loose 2-inch pieces
1 tablespoon finely sliced fresh chives
1 tablespoon finely chopped fresh Italian parsley
1 teaspoon fresh lemon juice
Small pinch red pepper flakes, optional
Small pinch flaky sea salt for finishing, optional
instructions: Using Baking Steel 72 Hour Pizza Dough Mix? Skip to step 13. Using ready-made dough? Skip to step 15 and use the prepared dough amount listed in the ingredients.

Two to three days before baking, make the scratch dough by whisking 195 grams cool water, 1/2 teaspoon honey, and 8 grams salt in a large mixing bowl until the honey dissolves and the salt is mostly dissolved.
Whisk the scant 1/8 teaspoon instant yeast, about 0.4 grams, into the salted honey water.
Add 285 grams high-protein bread flour and 15 grams whole wheat flour to the bowl with the yeasted water.
Mix with a dough scraper, spatula, or your hand for 1 to 2 minutes, until no dry flour remains and the dough looks shaggy.
Cover the bowl and rest the dough for 20 minutes so the flour hydrates before kneading.
Drizzle 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil over the dough.
Knead or fold the dough in the bowl for 3 to 4 minutes, until the oil is absorbed and the dough looks smoother but still slightly tacky.
Cover the bowl again and rest the dough at room temperature for 30 minutes.
With wet hands, stretch one side of the dough up and fold it over the center, then rotate the bowl and repeat 3 more times so all 4 sides have been folded.
Shape the dough into a tight ball, lightly coat a covered dough container or large zip-top bag with 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil, and place the dough inside.
Cover the dough and refrigerate it for 48 to 72 hours.

If using Baking Steel 72 Hour Pizza Dough Mix instead of the scratch dough, pour the mix into a bowl, add 1 3/4 cups water, knead for 2 to 3 minutes, rest the covered dough at room temperature for 12 to 24 hours, then refrigerate the covered dough for 2 days.
For the Baking Steel mix, portion 450 to 500 grams prepared dough about 3 hours before baking, shape that portion into a smooth ball, and save the remaining dough for another pizza or bread.
About 3 hours before baking, remove the cold dough from the refrigerator.
If the dough has spread in the container, gently tighten it back into a smooth ball without heavily kneading or degassing it.

Place the dough ball in a lightly oiled bowl or container, cover it, and let it warm at room temperature for about 3 hours, until relaxed, slightly puffy, and no longer refrigerator-cold in the center.
About 75 minutes before baking, place the Baking Steel Pro on the second-highest rack, ideally about 5 to 6 inches below the broiler element.
Preheat the oven to 550 degrees F for 60 to 75 minutes so the Baking Steel Pro is fully saturated with heat before the pizza goes in.
While the steel preheats, cook the onion by heating 1 tablespoon extra-virgin olive oil and 1 tablespoon unsalted butter in a large skillet over medium-low heat.
Add the thinly sliced medium onion, 1/4 teaspoon fine sea salt or Morton Coarse Kosher Salt, and 2 small thyme sprigs if using to the skillet.
Cook the onion for 20 to 25 minutes, stirring every 2 to 3 minutes, until the onion is golden brown, soft, and reduced to about 1/3 cup.

Stir 1 teaspoon sherry vinegar or red wine vinegar into the caramelized onion, scrape up any browned bits, cook for 30 seconds, discard the thyme sprigs if used, then transfer the onion to a small bowl to cool.
Wipe out the skillet if there are dark onion bits, then set the skillet over medium-high heat.
Add all 12 ounces sliced mushrooms to the dry skillet with 1/4 teaspoon fine sea salt or Morton Coarse Kosher Salt.
Cook the mushrooms for 6 to 8 minutes, stirring every minute, until they give off liquid and that liquid fully evaporates.
Add 1 tablespoon extra-virgin olive oil and 1 tablespoon unsalted butter to the dry cooked mushrooms.
Cook the mushrooms for 3 to 5 minutes more, stirring occasionally, until the edges are browned and the skillet smells nutty.
If using whole dried porcini mushrooms instead of porcini powder, grind enough dried porcini in a spice grinder or mortar and pestle to make 1 teaspoon fine powder.
Stir 1 teaspoon porcini powder, 1 finely grated small garlic clove, 1 teaspoon fresh thyme leaves, and 1/4 teaspoon black pepper into the browned mushrooms.
Cook the mushrooms for 30 to 45 seconds, just until the porcini, garlic, and thyme smell fragrant, then transfer the mushrooms to a plate to cool.
If the 1/3 cup whole-milk ricotta looks loose or wet, drain it in a fine-mesh strainer or on a double layer of paper towels for 15 minutes.

Make the white base by stirring together the drained 1/3 cup whole-milk ricotta, 2 tablespoons mascarpone, 1/2 ounce finely grated Pecorino Romano, 1 finely grated small garlic clove, 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil, 1/4 teaspoon finely grated lemon zest, 1/8 teaspoon black pepper, and a small pinch of fine sea salt or Morton Coarse Kosher Salt.
Taste the white base; it should be savory, garlicky, and lightly salty because it has to season the crust, mushrooms, onion, and cheese.
Freshly shred 5 ounces low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella from a block.
Finely dice or shred 3 ounces Italian fontina.
Toss together the 5 ounces shredded mozzarella, 3 ounces fontina, and 1/2 ounce finely grated Pecorino Romano so the cheese blend distributes evenly.
Keep the cheese blend cold until assembly so the cheese melts cleanly instead of oiling out on the peel.

Make the lemon-chive finishing oil by warming 1 tablespoon extra-virgin olive oil and 1 finely grated small garlic clove in a small saucepan over very low heat or in a microwave-safe bowl for 10 to 15 seconds, just until fragrant.

Stir 1 tablespoon sliced fresh chives, 1 tablespoon chopped fresh parsley, 1 teaspoon fresh lemon juice, the remaining 1/4 teaspoon finely grated lemon zest, 1/8 teaspoon black pepper, and a small pinch of red pepper flakes if using into the warm garlic oil.
Dust a 16-inch pizza peel with 1 tablespoon semolina flour or fine cornmeal.
Lightly flour the counter, place the room-temperature dough on the counter, and press from the center outward with your fingertips, leaving the outer 3/4 inch untouched and puffy.
Stretch the dough into a 15- to 16-inch round, keeping the center thin and the rim slightly thicker.
If the dough springs back while stretching, cover it and rest it for 5 minutes, then continue stretching.
Transfer the stretched dough to the semolina-dusted peel and gently shake the peel once to confirm the dough slides.
If any part sticks to the peel, lift that edge and dust a little more semolina flour or fine cornmeal underneath before topping.
Spread all of the white base over the dough in a very thin, even layer, leaving a 3/4-inch rim uncovered.
Scatter about two-thirds of the cold mozzarella-fontina-Pecorino blend evenly over the white base.
Scatter all of the cooled browned mushrooms evenly over the cheese, keeping heavy piles away from the center.
Dot all of the cooled caramelized onion across the mushrooms in small 1/2-teaspoon clusters so the onion seasons many slices instead of one wet patch.
Scatter the remaining mozzarella-fontina-Pecorino blend over any exposed mushrooms and onion.
Gently shake the peel again before launching the topped pizza; the pizza should slide freely as one piece.
If any spot sticks after topping, lift that edge with floured fingers or a bench scraper and dust a small pinch of semolina flour or fine cornmeal underneath.

Launch the pizza onto the fully preheated Baking Steel Pro as soon as it slides freely, ideally within 2 minutes of adding the white base, so moisture from the toppings does not glue the dough to the peel.
Bake the pizza at 550 degrees F for 2 1/2 minutes with the broiler off.
At 2 1/2 minutes, rotate the pizza 180 degrees with the peel so the back edge does not brown faster than the front edge.
Check the bottom crust by lifting one edge with the peel; at 2 1/2 minutes it should be set and lightly browned, not floppy, dark brown, or black.
If the bottom is still pale and flexible at 2 1/2 minutes, leave the pizza directly on the steel for 30 to 60 seconds more before using the broiler.
If the bottom already has dark brown spots at 2 1/2 minutes, slide the pizza onto a room-temperature rimless baking sheet before continuing so the bottom stops darkening on the steel.
Turn the broiler to high and broil for 30 to 75 seconds, watching constantly, until the rim blisters and the cheese bubbles with scattered golden spots.
If the rim darkens before the cheese is fully bubbling, turn the broiler off and finish at 550 degrees F for 30 to 90 seconds.

If the top still needs more color after 75 seconds under the broiler, turn the broiler off, return the oven to 550 degrees F, and bake for 1 to 2 minutes more while checking the bottom every 30 seconds.
If the bottom becomes deep golden before the top is finished, move the pizza onto the room-temperature rimless baking sheet and finish the top under the broiler for 30 to 60 seconds.
Transfer the baked pizza to a cutting board and let it rest for 2 minutes so the cheese settles and the crust stays crisp when sliced.
Tear 2 ounces thinly sliced speck or prosciutto into loose 2-inch pieces and drape the pieces over the hot pizza after the 2-minute rest.
Spoon or brush 2 to 3 teaspoons lemon-chive finishing oil over the speck, mushrooms, and exposed rim; avoid soaking the center of the pizza.
Sprinkle the remaining 1/2 ounce finely grated Pecorino Romano over the pizza.
Add a small pinch of flaky sea salt if the mushrooms or rim taste underseasoned.
Slice the pizza and serve immediately while the bottom crust is crisp, the rim is tender and blistered, and the speck is warm but not dried out.
notes: For more crust flavor and control, use the scratch dough and cold-ferment it for the full 72 hours. The small amount of whole wheat flour adds nutty flavor without making the large dough hard to stretch.
The scratch dough makes about one 500-gram dough ball, enough for a large 15- to 16-inch pizza with a crisp bottom, a tender rim, and enough structure for mushrooms, onion, and cheese.

If using a mix, Baking Steel 72 Hour Pizza Dough Mix is a good shortcut for this recipe because it is designed for a 72-hour cold ferment and uses organic Central Milling flour with only flour, salt, and yeast.
One prepared Baking Steel dough mix pack can make 3 larger pizzas if divided evenly. Use 450 to 500 grams prepared dough for this pizza and save the remaining dough for another bake.

If buying ready-made dough instead, ask a local pizzeria, Italian market, or bakery for 450 to 500 grams of cold-fermented dough made for a hot deck oven. Avoid canned dough, par-baked crusts, dough that smells sweet, dough that feels oily, or dough that tears immediately when stretched.
For home ovens at 550 degrees F, strong bread flour usually bakes better than pure Neapolitan 00 flour because it browns and crisps more reliably on a steel.
For the first bake, stretch to 15 or 15 1/2 inches instead of forcing the full 16 inches. The Baking Steel Pro is 16 x 16 inches, so a true 16-inch round leaves almost no launch margin.
Use a 16-inch peel for this pizza. A smaller peel makes a 15- to 16-inch dough difficult to launch cleanly.
Do not use parchment paper at 550 degrees F or under the broiler. A semolina-dusted peel gives a clean launch and avoids scorched paper edges.
Preheating the Baking Steel Pro for 60 to 75 minutes matters because the 3/8-inch steel needs time to heat through. A short preheat can leave the top cooked before the bottom crisps.
This white pizza has less wet tomato sauce than the other steel pizzas, so the bottom can brown faster. Start checking the bottom at 2 1/2 minutes.
Use the room-temperature rimless baking sheet if the bottom browns faster than the top. It lets the top finish without more direct steel contact.
The broiler is only for finishing. Watch constantly once the broiler is on because the rim, mushrooms, and cheese can go from blistered to burned quickly.
Cook the mushrooms until their liquid fully evaporates before adding butter. This keeps the pizza from turning watery and makes the mushrooms taste roasted instead of steamed.
Porcini powder adds concentrated mushroom flavor. Use 1 teaspoon for the first bake; more can make the mushrooms taste dusty or slightly bitter.
Caramelize the onion until it is reduced to about 1/3 cup. A smaller, darker amount tastes better on pizza than a large pile of pale onion.
Keep the white base thin. A heavy layer of ricotta and mascarpone can soften the center crust.

Use low-moisture whole-milk mozzarella from a block because it melts well without flooding the pizza. Avoid pre-shredded mozzarella if possible because anti-caking starches can make the melt drier and less glossy.
Italian fontina or fontina Val d'Aosta has clear flavor and melts well here. If you cannot find it, use Gruyere or young provolone rather than mild sandwich cheese.
Speck is added after baking so it stays tender and smoky instead of drying out under the broiler. Prosciutto works well if speck is unavailable.
The lemon-chive finishing oil goes on after baking because fresh herbs and lemon would lose their aroma and can scorch at 550 degrees F.
If the dough resists stretching, rest it for 5 minutes. Forcing tight dough knocks out gas and can make the rim dense.
If the bottom is too dark before the top is browned, use the room-temperature sheet sooner during that bake; next time, either move the steel one rack lower or start checking the bottom at 2 minutes.
If the top browns before the bottom crisps, keep the broiler off longer; next time, preheat the steel for the full 75 minutes and bake 1 rack position lower before using the broiler.
If the center is soft, cook the mushrooms drier, use 1 tablespoon less white base next time, and stretch the center thinner before topping.
Do not add truffle oil to this pizza. The mushrooms, fontina, speck, lemon, chives, and long-fermented crust provide plenty of flavor, and truffle oil can flatten those details.
Leftover lemon-chive finishing oil keeps covered in the refrigerator for 2 days. Warm it gently before brushing onto another hot crust.
title: Mushroom Speck Fontina White Pizza
totalTime: 1 hour 15 minutes active, plus 48 to 72 hours dough fermentation and 3 hours dough warm-up
